english: -- II. sbst. coat, clothing Sch.; *gón-če* (*gon che*) Lh., Ld., *gón-ma* (*gon ma*) Bal. id.
(spelling uncertain), *gob-nón čò-če* (*gob non co ce*), *táṅ-če* (*tang ce*), *gyáb-če* (*gyab ce*) W. to tease, vex, irritate.

1. a pace, step, góm-pa _bór-ba (gom pa 'bor ba) to make a step, to pace; góm-pa bdun _bór-ba (gom pa bdun 'bor ba) Glr. 5, 2 and elsewh.: to make seven steps, as a ceremony, which may also be counted equivalent to a religious pilgrimage, the actual performance of which is not possible: góm-pa _dór-ba (gom pa 'dor ba) prob. = _bór-ba ('bor ba); góm-pa _débs-pa (gom pa 'debs pa) and skyél-ba (skyel ba) Lt. ?? -- góm-pai stabs (gom pa'i stabs) the (peculiar) manner of stepping Zam.; *prú-gu-la gom-táṅ láb-če* (*pru gu la gom tang lab ce*) W. to teach a little child to walk; *gom čág-če* (*gom cag ce*) to stride solemnly along; *gom-jór* (*gom jor*) col a veranda (?). -- 2. the 'pas' in dancing.

accustomed, wonted, wont c. dat.; klóg-pa-la góms-šiṅ (klog pa la goms shing) practising (the art of) reading Dzl.; góms-par byéd-pa (goms par byed pa), and _gyúr-ba ('gyur ba) c. dat. and accus., to accustom one's self to a thing, to practise; mi góm-pa (mi gom pa) unaccustomed; *mi daṅ góm-te* (*mi dang gom te*) W. accustomed to man, tame, domesticated: *ghom-kyé* (*ghom kye*) C. a habit, custom.
Cs.: a general name for stone; Sch.: stones, rubble, bowlder-stones.

eleg. gor-ma-bkúm (gor ma bkum), certain, sure, indubitable; de _byúṅ-ba gor-ma-čág-go (de 'byung ba gor ma chag go) his coming is quite sure Wdṅ.; dé yin gor-ma-čág-go (de yin gor ma chag go) that it is this, is quite certain Stg. frq; gor-ma-čág-par (gor ma chag par) adv. certainly.
1. round, circular Sch.; gor-gór (gor gor) Med. id. -- 2. W. a rupee.

1. resp. ná-bza (na bza'), garment, dress. -- 2. in some compounds silk. -- rgyán-gos (rgyan gos) fine clothes Glr.; rgyún-gos (rgyun gos) C., W., an every day coat; čos-gos (chos gos) clerical garb or garments Schl. 170, Burn. I. 306, Köpp. I. 339, II. 266; mtán-gos (mtan gos) a sort of petticoat worn by the monks, having many plaits and folds, like the kilt of the Highlanders, but longer and of one colour; pó-gos (pho gos) man's dress, blá-gos (bla gos) an upper garment, a kind of toga; mó-gos (mo gos) a woman's gown; } záb-gos (zab gos) holiday clothes, opp. to rgyun-gos (rgyun gos) C., W. -- gos gón-pa (gos gon pa), gyón-pa (gyon pa) to put on, _búd-pa ('bud pa) to take off, brjè-ba (brje ba) to change clothes; brtségs-pa (brtsegs pa) Sch.: to put one garment over the other; gos btég-pa (gos bteg pa) to tuck up, by drawing the front skirts under the girdle; gos ldáb-pa (gos ldab pa) to lay or fold a coat together; gos spú-ma (gos spu ma) a coat of napped cloth. Comp. gos-skúd (gos skud) silk-thread. -- gos-sgáb (gos sgab) skirt or flap of a coat. -- gos-sgám (gos sgam) box, chest, or press for clothes, wardrobe. -- gos-čén (gos chen), col. go-šén (go shen), silk fabrics, silks. -- gos-rnyíṅ (gos rnying) an old coat or dress. -- gos-túṅ (gos thung) trowsers Glr., C. -- gos-mtá (gos mtha') = gos-sgab (gos sgab). -- gos-nág (gos nag) a black garment, a female dress. -- gos-bzáṅ (gos bzang) a beautiful dress, fine clothes (as an object of show), festival raiment. gos-lág (gos lag) (in W. also pronounced *goi-lág* (*goi lag*), *go-lág* (*go lag*) in C. *gho-lág* (*gho lag*)) dress, clothes, body-linen; *gos-lág ṭú-če* (*gos lag thru ce*) W. to wash linen.
num. instead of brgyád-ču (brgyad cu), in the abbreviated numbers gya-gcíg (gya gcig) etc. 81-89.

a root, the meaning of which is not quite settled yet; it occurs in the following combinations: gya-gyú (gya gyu) (Cs.: crookedness?) intrigues, secret machinations C., W.; gnód-séms (gnod sems) daṅ (dang) bslú-bai (bslu ba'i) gya-gyú (gya gyu) sogs (sogs) malice, deceitful tricks and the like; gya-gyu-čan (gya gyu can) crafty, deceitful, fraudulent, e.g. sems (sems); gya-gyú byéd-pa (gya gyu byed pa) to intrigue, to plot. -- gya-ma-gyú (gya ma gyu) 1. of rivers etc.: quiet, calm, gently flowing along Mil. 2. of a man: cautious, close, reserved, so that one does not know what to think of him, ni f. -- gya-nyés (gya nyes) Mil. was explained: marvelous, inexplicable, of men, occurrences etc. -- gya-nóm-pa (gya nom pa) Cs.: 'contentment, joy'; yet the context in several passages of Mil. suggests the signification: abundance, sufficiency. -- gya-rtsóm (gya rtsom), gya-tsóm (gya tsom) haste, hurry, rashness Cs.
